title: The British Journal of Sociology in the 1960s: a discipline in ferment
abstract: Early on in the process of reading all of the articles published by the BJS in the 1960s, a period of great ferment in sociology, I abandoned any hope of singling out the two ‘best’ articles from that decade. Depending on how you choose to define best, virtually every issue contains one or two pieces that might reasonably contend for that title. Instead, I decided to capitalize on the benefit of hindsight by looking for articles by young sociologists destined to go on to illustrious careers in which they set out a scholarly agenda that would open up new fields of research and forge their sociological identities in the decades to come. Even employing these strict inclusion criteria, my reading turned up a number of meritorious candidates for consideration. But I could only select two and, in the end, the choices were fairly easy: Peter Townsend's‘The meaning of poverty’ (1962) and Frances Heidensohn's‘The deviance of women’ (1968). Beyond their enduring scholarly influence, there were other reasons that these two selections seemed to me to be especially fitting for this volume. Townsend's article was published near the beginning of the decade and Heidensohn's appeared near its close, so together they nicely bookended the period. I also thought it particularly appropriate that one of the pieces was authored by a man and the other a woman as the seeds of our increased – if still far from perfect – present day sensitivity to matters of gender equity were sown in the 1960s. At the time I selected their articles, in mid-2008, both Townsend and Heidensohn remained actively engaged in the sociological enterprise, still highly productive scholars committed to the continuation of a research agenda that had defined their careers from the start. Accordingly, I decided to ask each of them to write a brief commentary to accompany the republication of their article in which they reflected on the socio-cultural context in which it was written. Both agreed to do so. Sadly, though, Townsend died unexpectedly before completing his commentary. Fortunately, Jake Rosenfeld, a young American sociologist, has contributed a very thoughtful commentary in which he assesses the legacy of Townsend's early article. This piece stands as a fine tribute to one of the giants of British sociology, a deeply committed social scientist who never lost sight of the real world implications of his research. I think that readers will be fascinated by Heidensohn's nuanced reflections on the writing of her path-breaking article, which played a key role in establishing feminist sociological criminology. That is what prompted me to select Heidensohn's article despite the fact that she currently serves as General Editor of the BJS. It is worth stressing that Heidensohn played no part in this process. Indeed, she insisted that I seek approval from the Editorial Board before reluctantly agreeing to its inclusion. Heidensohn's commentary is joined by one from Jody Miller, an eminent American feminist sociological criminologist, who considers the enduring impact of Heidensohn's call for an end to the androcentrism that characterized sociology in the 1960s (and beyond). As with the Rosenfeld commentary on Townsend, Miller's essay serves to remind us that, in the words of Isaac Newton, we stand on the shoulders of giants.
